title: Beef madras
subtitle: Hot, sour beef curry with South Indian spicing
excerpt: Beef braised until tender in a fiery, tamarind-soured sauce of reduced tomatoes and South Indian-influenced spicing. Beef madras follows the same formula as lamb madras (high chilli, tamarind sourness, dark thick sauce) but the longer cooking time required for beef produces a richer, more gelatinous result. A slow-cook curry at the hot end of the heat scale.
sourceNotes: Madras curry is a British-Indian restaurant heat category. The beef version applies the same South Indian-influenced spicing to beef braising cuts. Documented on British Indian menus from the 1970s as a standard heat-category variant. Braising beef suits the long cooking required better than quicker cuts.

content.text: Braising beef takes longer than lamb to become tender, allow at least 90 minutes at a proper simmer. Chuck or braising steak contains enough collagen that it becomes almost silky at full tenderness. If the beef is still stringy or resistant at 90 minutes, it hasn't been cooked long enough; add more water and continue. Beef also benefits more than lamb from making a day ahead: the sauce penetrates the meat further as it cools and reheats.

Cook the tomato base down to a thick paste before you add the water. This is the same key step as in lamb madras. It matters even more here. The braise runs longer, and a concentrated base keeps the flavour through the extended cook.
Brown the beef and reduce the base

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a large heavy pan over high heat. Brown the {{braising-steak}} in batches, 3 minutes per batch, without crowding. Remove and set aside. Reduce heat to medium. Add the {{onion}} and cook for 12 to 15 minutes until deep golden. Add the {{garlic}}, {{ginger-root}}, {{turmeric}}, {{cumin-ground}}, {{coriander-ground}}, and {{chilli-powder}} and stir for 1 minute. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} and cook for 12 minutes, stirring regularly, until reduced to a thick paste.

Return the beef to the pan. Add the {{tamarind-paste}} and 300 ml water. Bring to the boil, cover, and reduce to a low simmer. Cook for 80 to 90 minutes until the beef is tender, stirring every 20 minutes. Remove the lid for the final 15 minutes to reduce the sauce to a thick, coating consistency. Stir in the {{garam-masala}} and season with salt.

items.fix: Chuck and braising steak are the correct cuts. They have sufficient collagen to soften. If using a leaner cut, add another 30 minutes and check. The liquid should be at a visible simmer throughout
items.cause: Temperature too low or cut too lean

items.fix: Add 100 ml more water and reduce the heat. Check every 20 minutes and add water as needed to keep a thin layer of liquid in the pan
items.cause: Heat too high or liquid evaporated too quickly
items.symptom: Sauce burning before beef is done

items.fix: Add another half tablespoon of tamarind paste in the last 10 minutes of cooking, after the sauce has reduced
items.cause: Tamarind insufficient or diluted by extra water

items.fix: Skim the surface with a spoon during cooking, or refrigerate and remove the solidified fat from the top before reheating
items.cause: Beef fat rendering into the sauce during the long braise

content.text: Beef madras is less common in British curry-houses than lamb or chicken madras. Beef is less used in South Asian restaurant cooking in general. The long braise also makes it harder to cook to order. Cooked properly, the meat softens and the sauce has time to develop. The dish has a depth and richness the quicker lamb version cannot match. Plan ahead and the wait pays off.
